qtype: ratio sharing, percentage decrease, reverse percentage
tex:
(a) Ali and Mo share a sum of money in the ratio Ali : Mo = $9 : 7$.
\newline
\\
Ali receives \$600 more than Mo.
\newline
\\
Calculate how much each receives.
\newline

(b) In a sale, Ali buys a television for \$195.80.
\newline
\\
The original price was \$220.
\newline
\\
Calculate the percentage reduction on the original price.
\newline

(c) In the sale, Mo buys a jacket for \$63.
\newline
\\
The original price was reduced by 25\%.
\newline
\\
Calculate the original price of the jacket.
\newline

content.content:
Ali and Mo share a sum of money in the ratio Ali : Mo = $9 : 7$.
\newline
\\
Ali receives \$600 more than Mo.
\newline
\\
Calculate how much each receives.
\newline

In a sale, Ali buys a television for \$195.80.
\newline
\\
The original price was \$220.
\newline
\\
Calculate the percentage reduction on the original price.
\newline

In the sale, Mo buys a jacket for \$63.
\newline
\\
The original price was reduced by 25\%.
\newline
\\
Calculate the original price of the jacket.
\newline

_rawBody:
\begin{parts}
\item Ali and Mo share a sum of money in the ratio Ali : Mo = $9 : 7$.\\
Ali receives \$600 more than Mo.\\
Calculate how much each receives.

\AnswerLine[Ali \$][]
\AnswerLine[Mo \$][]
\Marks{3}

\item In a sale, Ali buys a television for \$195.80.\\
The original price was \$220.\\
Calculate the percentage reduction on the original price.

\AnswerLine[][\%]
\Marks{3}

\item In the sale, Mo buys a jacket for \$63.\\
The original price was reduced by 25\%.\\
Calculate the original price of the jacket.

\AnswerLine[\$][]
\Marks{3}

\end{parts}

qtype: compound interest, exponential growth, reverse exponential growth, finding time in exponential growth
tex:
(a) Dina invests \$600 for 5 years at a rate of 2\% per year compound interest.
\newline
\\
Calculate the value of this investment at the end of the 5 years.
\newline

(b) The value of a gold ring increases exponentially at a rate of 5\% per year.
\newline
\\
The value is now \$882.
\newline

(i) (c) Calculate the value of the ring 2 years ago.
\newline

(d) Find the number of complete years it takes for the ring's value of \$882 to increase to a value greater than \$1100.
\newline

content.content:
Dina invests \$600 for 5 years at a rate of 2\% per year compound interest.
\newline
\\
Calculate the value of this investment at the end of the 5 years.
\newline

The value of a gold ring increases exponentially at a rate of 5\% per year.
\newline
\\
The value is now \$882.
\newline

Calculate the value of the ring 2 years ago.
\newline

Find the number of complete years it takes for the ring's value of \$882 to increase to a value greater than \$1100.
\newline

tex:
(a) (i) (b) Calculate the external curved surface area of a cylinder with radius 8 m and height 19 m.
\newline

(c) This surface is painted at a cost of \$0.85 per square metre.
\newline
\\
Calculate the cost of painting this surface.
\newline

(d) A solid metal sphere with radius 6 cm is melted down and all of the metal is used to make a solid cone with radius 8 cm and height $h$ cm.
\newline

(i) (e) Show that $h = 13.5$.
\newline
[The volume, $V$, of a sphere with radius $r$ is $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$.
\newline
]
[The volume, $V$, of a cone with radius $r$ and height $h$ is $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$.
\newline
]

(f) Calculate the slant height of the cone.
\newline

(g) Calculate the curved surface area of the cone.
\newline
\\ \null
[The curved surface area, $A$, of a cone with radius $r$ and slant height $l$ is $A = \pi rl$.
\newline
]

(h) Two cones are mathematically similar.
\newline
\\
The total surface area of the smaller cone is 80 cm$^2$.
\newline
\\
The total surface area of the larger cone is 180 cm$^2$.
\newline
\\
The volume of the smaller cone is 168 cm$^3$.
\newline
\\
Calculate the volume of the larger cone.
\newline

\\
$DB = 8$ cm.
\newline
\\
$P$ is vertically above the centre, $X$, of the base and $PX = 5$ cm.
\newline

Calculate the angle between $PB$ and the base $ABCD$.
\newline

tex:
The diagram shows a triangular field, $ABC$, on horizontal ground.
\newline

(a) Olav runs from $A$ to $B$ at a constant speed of 4 m/s and then from $B$ to $C$ at a constant speed of 3 m/s.
\newline
\\
He then runs at a constant speed from $C$ to $A$.
\newline
\\
His average speed for the whole journey is 3.6 m/s.
\newline

Calculate his speed when he runs from $C$ to $A$.
\newline

(b) Use the cosine rule to find angle $BAC$.
\newline

(c) The bearing of $C$ from $A$ is 210°.
\newline

(i) (d) Find the bearing of $B$ from $A$.
\newline

(e) Find the bearing of $A$ from $B$.
\newline

(f) $D$ is the point on $AC$ that is nearest to $B$.
\newline
\\
Calculate the distance from $D$ to $A$.
\newline

\newline
\\
He then runs at a constant speed from $C$ to $A$.
\newline
\\

\newline

\newline

\newline

\newline

\newline

\newline

\newline
\\
Calculate the distance from $D$ to $A$.
\newline

tex:
(a) The cumulative frequency diagram shows information about the times taken by 200 students to solve a problem.
\newline

Use the cumulative frequency diagram to find an estimate for

(i) (b) the median,

(c) the interquartile range,

(d) the number of students who took more than 40 minutes.
\newline

(e) Roberto records the value of each of the coins he has at home.
\newline
\\
The table shows the results.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
Value (cents) & 1 & 2 & 5 & 10 & 20 & 50 \\ \hline
Frequency & 3 & 1 & 3 & 2 & 4 & 2 \\ \hline
\end{tabular}
(i) (f) Find the range.
\newline

(g) Find the mode.
\newline

(h) Find the median.
\newline

(i) Work out the total value of Roberto's coins.
\newline

(j) Work out the mean.
\newline

(k) The histogram shows information about the masses of 100 boxes.
\newline

Calculate an estimate of the mean.
\newline

content.content:
The cumulative frequency diagram shows information about the times taken by 200 students to solve a problem.
\newline
Use the cumulative frequency diagram to find an estimate for
the median,
the interquartile range,

the number of students who took more than 40 minutes.
\newline

Roberto records the value of each of the coins he has at home.
\newline
\\
The table shows the results.
\newline

Find the range.
\newline

Find the mode.
\newline

Find the median.
\newline

Work out the total value of Roberto's coins.
\newline

Work out the mean.
\newline

The histogram shows information about the masses of 100 boxes.
\newline

Calculate an estimate of the mean.
\newline

qtype: forming and solving linear and simultaneous equations, setting up and simplifying fractional algebraic equations
tex:
(a) Oranges cost 21 cents each.
\newline
\\
Alex buys $x$ oranges and Bobbie buys $(x + 2)$ oranges.
\newline
\\
The total cost of these oranges is \$4.20.
\newline

Find the value of $x$.
\newline

(b) The cost of one ruler is $r$ cents.
\newline
\\
The cost of one protractor is $p$ cents.
\newline

The total cost of 5 rulers and 1 protractor is 245 cents.
\newline
\\
The total cost of 2 rulers and 3 protractors is 215 cents.
\newline

Write down two equations in terms of $r$ and $p$ and solve these equations to find the cost of one protractor.
\newline

(c) Carol walks 12 km at $x$ km/h and then a further 6 km at $(x - 1)$ km/h.
\newline
\\
The total time taken is 5 hours.
\newline

(i) (d) Write an equation, in terms of $x$, and show that it simplifies to $5x^2 - 23x + 12 = 0$.
\newline

(e) Factorise $5x^2 - 23x + 12$.
\newline

(f) Solve the equation $5x^2 - 23x + 12 = 0$.
\newline

{...}]

(g) Write down Carol's walking speed during the final 6 km.
\newline

content.content:
Oranges cost 21 cents each.
\newline
\\
Alex buys $x$ oranges and Bobbie buys $(x + 2)$ oranges.
\newline
\\
The total cost of these oranges is \$4.20.
\newline

Find the value of $x$.
\newline

The cost of one ruler is $r$ cents.
\newline
\\
The cost of one protractor is $p$ cents.
\newline

The total cost of 5 rulers and 1 protractor is 245 cents.
\newline
\\
The total cost of 2 rulers and 3 protractors is 215 cents.
\newline

Write down two equations in terms of $r$ and $p$ and solve these equations to find the cost of one protractor.
\newline

Carol walks 12 km at $x$ km/h and then a further 6 km at $(x - 1)$ km/h.
\newline
\\
The total time taken is 5 hours.
\newline

Write an equation, in terms of $x$, and show that it simplifies to $5x^2 - 23x + 12 = 0$.
\newline

Factorise $5x^2 - 23x + 12$.
\newline

Solve the equation $5x^2 - 23x + 12 = 0$.
\newline

Write down Carol's walking speed during the final 6 km.
\newline

tex:
The diagram shows 5 cards.
\newline

(a) Donald chooses a card at random.
\newline

(i) (b) Write down the probability that the number of dots on this card is an even number.
\newline

(c) Write down the probability that the number of dots on this card is a prime number.
\newline

(d) Donald chooses two of the five cards at random, without replacement.
\newline
\\
He works out the total number of dots on these two cards.
\newline

(i) (e) Find the probability that the total number of dots is 5.
\newline

(f) Find the probability that the total number of dots is an odd number.
\newline

\newline

\newline

(c) 

By shading the unwanted regions on the grid, show and label the region $R$ that satisfies all four inequalities.
\newline

(d) (i) (e) Find the number of small cars and the number of large cars needed to carry exactly 60 people.
\newline

{...} large cars]

\newline

tex:
(a) Complete the table for the 5th term and the $n$th term of each sequence.
\newline

\begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
1st term & 2nd term & 3rd term & 4th term & 5th term & \dots & $n$th term \\ \hline
9 & 5 & 1 & $-3$ & & & \\ \hline
4 & 9 & 16 & 25 & & & \\ \hline
1 & 8 & 27 & 64 & & & \\ \hline
8 & 16 & 32 & 64 & & & \\ \hline
\end{tabular}

(b) $0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, \dots$\\
This sequence is a Fibonacci sequence.
\newline
\\
After the first two terms, the rule to find the next term is ``add the two previous terms''.
\newline
\\
For example, $5 + 8 = 13$.
\newline
\\
Use this rule to complete each of the following Fibonacci sequences.
\newline

(i) (c) $2, \quad 4, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots$

(d) $1, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad 11$

(e) $\dots\dots, \quad -1, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad 1$

(f) $\dfrac{1}{3}, \dfrac{3}{4}, \dfrac{4}{7}, \dfrac{7}{11}, \dfrac{11}{18}, \dots$

(i) (g) One term of this sequence is $\dfrac{p}{q}$.
\newline
\\
Find, in terms of $p$ and $q$, the next term in this sequence.
\newline

(h) Find the 6th term of this sequence.
\newline

content.content:
Complete the table for the 5th term and the $n$th term of each sequence.
\newline

$0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, \dots$\\
This sequence is a Fibonacci sequence.
\newline
\\
After the first two terms, the rule to find the next term is ``add the two previous terms''.
\newline
\\
For example, $5 + 8 = 13$.
\newline
\\
Use this rule to complete each of the following Fibonacci sequences.
\newline
$2, \quad 4, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots$
$1, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad 11$
$\dots\dots, \quad -1, \quad \dots\dots, \quad \dots\dots, \quad 1$

One term of this sequence is $\dfrac{p}{q}$.
\newline
\\
Find, in terms of $p$ and $q$, the next term in this sequence.
\newline

Find the 6th term of this sequence.
\newline
